question: For which kind of column is a BITMAP index most suitable?
options: A high-cardinality column (many distinct values) in an OLTP system
A low-cardinality column (few distinct values) in a read-mostly / data-warehouse system

explanation.uz: Bitmap indeks kam kardinallikli (kam xil qiymatli, masalan jins, holat) ustunlar va ko'p o'qiladigan (data warehouse) tizimlar uchun eng mos. OLTP'da yozuvlar ko'p bo'lsa, bitmap lock muammosi kelib chiqadi — u yerda B-tree afzal.
explanation.en: A bitmap index suits low-cardinality columns (few distinct values, e.g. gender, status) in read-mostly / data-warehouse systems. In OLTP with frequent writes, bitmap locking is a problem — a B-tree is preferred there.

question:
Why might this query NOT use the index on `name`?

```sql
SELECT * FROM emp WHERE UPPER(name) = 'ALI';
```
options: Applying a function to an indexed column disables the normal index (unless a function-based index exists)
UPPER is not a valid function

explanation.uz: Indekslangan ustunga funksiya qo'llaganda (UPPER(name)) oddiy indeks ishlamaydi — optimizer full scan qiladi. Yechim: shu ifoda uchun function-based index yaratish yoki so'rovni qayta yozish.
explanation.en: Wrapping an indexed column in a function (UPPER(name)) prevents the normal index from being used — the optimizer does a full scan. Fix: create a function-based index on that expression, or rewrite the query.

question: You have a composite index on (last_name, first_name). Which query can use it MOST efficiently?
options: A query with no WHERE clause

explanation.uz: Kompozit indeksda BOSHLOVCHI ustun (last_name) shart bo'lsa, indeks samarali ishlaydi. Faqat first_name bo'yicha qidiruv boshlovchi ustunni o'tkazib yuboradi (index skip scan bundan istisno, lekin cheklangan).
explanation.en: A composite index works efficiently when the LEADING column (last_name) is in the predicate. Searching by first_name alone skips the leading column (index skip scan is an exception, but limited).

question: When is a FULL TABLE SCAN often BETTER than using an index?
options: When the query returns a small number of rows
When the query returns a large percentage of the table's rows

explanation.uz: So'rov jadvalning katta qismini (masalan >5-10%) qaytarsa, full scan indeks orqali bitta-bitta o'qishdan tezroq bo'ladi (multiblock read). Indeks kam satr qaytarilganda afzal.
explanation.en: If a query returns a large fraction of the table (e.g. >5-10%), a full scan (multiblock reads) is often faster than many single-row index lookups. Indexes win when few rows are returned.

question: What is a downside of having many indexes on a table?
options: INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE become slower because every index must be maintained

explanation.uz: Indekslar o'qishni tezlashtiradi, lekin har DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) da barcha tegishli indekslar ham yangilanishi kerak — bu yozishni sekinlashtiradi va joy oladi. Shuning uchun kerakli indekslarni tanlab yaratish kerak.
explanation.en: Indexes speed up reads, but every DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) must also maintain all relevant indexes — slowing writes and using space. So create only the indexes you need.

question: What is a FUNCTION-BASED index used for?
options: Indexing the result of an expression/function so queries using that expression can use the index

explanation.uz: Function-based index ifoda/funksiya natijasini indekslaydi — masalan CREATE INDEX ... ON emp(UPPER(name)). Shundan so'ng WHERE UPPER(name)=... so'rovi indeksdan foydalana oladi.
explanation.en: A function-based index indexes the result of an expression/function — e.g. CREATE INDEX ... ON emp(UPPER(name)). Then WHERE UPPER(name)=... can use the index.

question: What is an optimizer HINT, e.g. /*+ INDEX(e emp_idx) */ ?
options: An instruction that influences the optimizer's plan choice (e.g. forcing an index)
A way to create an index

explanation.uz: Hint — optimizer'ga reja tanlashda ta'sir qiladigan maxsus izoh (/*+ ... */). Masalan, ma'lum indeksni ishlatishga majbur qiladi. Ehtiyotkorlik bilan ishlatish kerak — statistika yaxshi bo'lsa, CBO odatda o'zi to'g'ri tanlaydi.
explanation.en: A hint is a special comment (/*+ ... */) that influences the optimizer's plan choice — e.g. forcing a specific index. Use sparingly — with good statistics, the CBO usually chooses well on its own.

question: How does PARTITIONING a large table improve query performance?
options: Partition pruning lets the optimizer scan only the relevant partitions instead of the whole table

explanation.uz: Katta jadvalni bo'laklarga (partition) ajratsak, so'rov shartiga mos partition pruning tufayli optimizer faqat kerakli bo'laklarni o'qiydi — butun jadvalni emas. Bu I/O ni keskin kamaytiradi.
explanation.en: Splitting a large table into partitions lets partition pruning read only the relevant partitions matching the query, not the whole table. This sharply cuts I/O.

question: How does a MATERIALIZED VIEW differ from a normal VIEW for performance?
options: A materialized view STORES precomputed results on disk (fast reads); a normal view re-runs its query each time
A normal view stores data; a materialized view does not

explanation.uz: Materialized view so'rov natijasini diskda SAQLAYDI (oldindan hisoblab) — takroriy og'ir hisob-kitoblar (masalan agregatlar) tez o'qiladi. Oddiy view esa har murojaatda asosiy so'rovni qayta bajaradi. Materialized view yangilanib turishi (refresh) kerak.
explanation.en: A materialized view STORES precomputed results on disk — heavy repeated computations (e.g. aggregates) are read fast. A normal view re-runs its query each time. A materialized view must be refreshed to stay current.

question: Which WHERE predicate is 'sargable' (index-friendly)?
options: WHERE hire_date >= DATE '2020-01-01' AND hire_date < DATE '2020-01-02'

explanation.uz: Sargable predikat — indekslangan ustunni funksiyaga o'ramaydigan shart. Ustunga funksiya (TRUNC, SUBSTR) yoki arifmetika qo'llasak, indeks ishlamaydi. Diapazon (>= ... < ...) ko'rinishda yozsak, indeks ishlaydi.
explanation.en: A sargable predicate doesn't wrap the indexed column in a function. Applying a function (TRUNC, SUBSTR) or arithmetic to the column disables the index. Rewriting as a range (>= ... < ...) keeps the index usable.

question: To diagnose overall database performance and find the biggest bottlenecks, a DBA typically looks at:
options: AWR/ASH reports and wait events (V$ views)

explanation.uz: DBA umumiy unumdorlikni AWR/ASH hisobotlari va kutish hodisalari (wait events, V$SESSION/V$SYSTEM_EVENT) orqali tahlil qiladi — qaysi resurs (CPU, I/O, lock) eng ko'p kutish keltirayotganini topadi.
explanation.en: A DBA diagnoses overall performance via AWR/ASH reports and wait events (V$SESSION/V$SYSTEM_EVENT) — finding which resource (CPU, I/O, locks) causes the most waiting.

question: For checking 'does a matching row exist in another table', which is often the most efficient?
options: A correlated EXISTS subquery (stops at the first match)
Selecting all rows and counting in the app

explanation.uz: Mavjudlikni tekshirishda `EXISTS` odatda samarali — u BIRINCHI mos satr topilganda to'xtaydi, hammasini sanamaydi. COUNT(*) esa barcha mos satrlarni sanashga majbur bo'ladi (keraksiz ish).
explanation.en: For existence checks, `EXISTS` is usually efficient — it stops at the FIRST matching row instead of counting all. COUNT(*) forces counting every match (wasted work).
